section.title: Bless the LORD, You Servants
section.sectionRationale: The last of the fifteen Songs of Ascents, Psalms 120 to 134, this closing pilgrim song sends worshipers into the night with a call to bless the LORD and a benediction in return, forming the fitting final word of the whole Ascents collection.
section.thesis: Worship is not bound to daylight or festival crowds; the night watch servants of the LORD are called to bless Him continually, and He answers, blessing His people in return from Zion, the Maker of heaven and earth.
section.frameNote: One short movement in three lines: the call to the night watch servants to bless the LORD (v1), the physical gesture of lifted hands (v2), and the LORD's answering blessing from Zion (v3).

verses.text: Come, bless the LORD, all you servants of the LORD who serve by night in the house of the LORD!
Lift up your hands to the sanctuary and {{barak|bless}} the LORD!
May the LORD, the Maker of heaven and earth, bless you from Zion.

barak.gloss: To bless, kneel, or praise. The same root describes both the people blessing the LORD in worship and the LORD blessing His people in return, a two way exchange of covenant favor.

basic.context: The final Song of Ascents, sung as pilgrims' festival visit to Jerusalem drew to a close. It turns from the pilgrims themselves to address the temple servants who kept watch through the night, urging them to keep worship going long after the daytime crowds had gone home.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm describes a specific liturgical exchange between departing worshipers and night watch priests, and by extension calls every generation to see that God's praise is not limited to daylight, festival, or public settings.
basic.originalLanguages: The single Hebrew root barak, to bless, carries the whole psalm: worshipers bless the LORD with lifted hands, and the LORD in turn blesses them from Zion, showing worship as a genuine two way exchange rather than a one directional demand.
basic.typology: The night watch in the earthly temple anticipates the call to unceasing prayer and praise in the church, a sacrifice of praise offered continually, Hebrews 13:15, and looks forward to the new creation where God's presence needs no night at all, Revelation 22:5.
basic.christ: Christ is greater than the temple, Matthew 12:6, and in Him access to God's presence and blessing is no longer confined to the sanctuary or its hours; through Him believers themselves become a holy priesthood offering spiritual sacrifices continually, 1 Peter 2:5, 9.
basic.god: God is presented as the Maker of heaven and earth, fully able to bless from Zion those who bless Him, tying His cosmic power directly to His covenant faithfulness toward His worshiping people.
basic.crossReferences: Numbers 6:24-26 gives the priestly blessing this psalm echoes in reverse. Hebrews 13:15 calls believers to a sacrifice of praise offered continually. 1 Peter 2:5 and 9 names believers a holy priesthood. Revelation 22:5 pictures worship needing no night at all.
basic.oneStory: The night watch blessing of the earthly temple anticipates the church's calling to unceasing praise in Christ, and points forward to the new creation, where God's people will serve and bless Him forever in unbroken day.

context.literary: A brief, three verse psalm shaped as a call and response, a plural call to the night watch servants in verses 1 to 2, answered by a singular blessing spoken back to the worshipers in verse 3, forming an inclusio of blessing given and blessing received.
context.historical: Levites and priests kept watch over the temple day and night, Exodus 30:7-8, 1 Chronicles 9:27, burning incense and tending the altar so morning sacrifice could begin at dawn; this psalm gives voice to those unseen night hours of service.
context.canonical: The final Song of Ascents, closing the collection begun in Psalm 120 and immediately following Psalm 133's picture of unity, so that unity and continual blessing together form the last word of the pilgrim songs.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To call the temple's night watch servants to bless the LORD even in the unseen hours, and to assure departing pilgrims of an answering blessing from the Maker of heaven and earth.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific temple practice of night worship; it prescribes as an abiding pattern that praise to God should not be limited to public, daylight, or festival settings, but should continue at all hours.

debates.question: Who speaks verse 3, the departing pilgrims or the officiating priest?

views.label: The priest or Levite pronounces the blessing back on the departing pilgrims
views.case: The shift from plural address, verses 1-2, to a singular blessing in verse 3 fits a single priestly voice answering the crowd, similar to the priestly blessing of Numbers 6:24-26.

views.label: The pilgrims themselves voice a mutual blessing back on the night watch servants
views.case: The psalm's brevity and its place among pilgrim songs could suggest the traveling worshipers speaking a parting blessing over those who serve through the night on their behalf.

debates.bearing: Either reading preserves the psalm's central point, that blessing flows both ways between worshipers and those who serve God's house, modeling mutual encouragement in worship.

study.originalLanguages: The verb barak, to bless, ties the whole psalm together: the night watch servants bless the LORD with lifted hands, and the LORD, named here Maker of heaven and earth, blesses His people back from Zion, showing that true worship always moves in both directions.

typology.type: The night watch servants who never let temple praise fall silent
typology.fulfillment: The church's calling to a sacrifice of praise offered continually through Christ, Hebrews 13:15, and to unceasing prayer, 1 Thessalonians 5:17.

typology.type: The temple as the place where blessing is given and received
typology.fulfillment: Christ, greater than the temple, Matthew 12:6, through whom believers become a holy priesthood with constant access to God, 1 Peter 2:5, 9.

study.christ: Jesus declares Himself greater than the temple, Matthew 12:6, and through His finished work believers no longer need a sanctuary's walls or fixed hours to bless God; they are made a holy priesthood offering spiritual sacrifices continually, 1 Peter 2:5, 9, anticipating the new creation where God's people serve Him without any night at all, Revelation 22:3-5.
study.god: God is named the Maker of heaven and earth, the same cosmic Creator who condescends to bless ordinary worshipers from Zion in response to their praise, showing that His transcendence and His nearness to His people are held together, not in tension.

crossReferences.note: The priestly blessing this psalm's closing benediction echoes, God blessing His people by name.
Calls believers to offer a sacrifice of praise to God continually, echoing this psalm's night and day worship.
Names believers a holy priesthood, extending the temple servants' calling to all God's people in Christ.
Pictures God's servants worshiping Him in the new creation where there is no more night.

study.oneStory: The unseen night watch praise of the earthly temple anticipates the church's calling in Christ to unceasing worship, and points forward to the new creation, where God's people, made a holy priesthood, will bless Him forever in a day that never ends.

quiz.q: Who does Psalm 134 specifically call to bless the LORD?

options.text: The servants of the LORD who serve by night in His house.
options.note: Verse 1 names exactly this group, the temple's night watch.

options.text: Foreign kings and their armies.
options.note: The psalm addresses temple servants, not foreign rulers.

options.text: Only the high priest alone.
options.note: The call is plural, to 'all you servants,' not to one individual priest.

options.text: Farmers bringing their harvest offerings.
options.note: The psalm's setting is the temple's night watch, not agricultural offerings.

quiz.q: What physical gesture does verse 2 call for in worship?

options.text: Lifting up hands to the sanctuary.
options.note: Verse 2 explicitly commands, 'Lift up your hands to the sanctuary and bless the LORD.'

options.text: Bowing the face to the ground in silence.
options.note: Other psalms describe bowing, but this verse specifically names lifted hands.

options.text: Removing sandals as at holy ground.
options.note: This gesture belongs to Moses at the burning bush, not to this psalm.

options.text: Clapping in rhythm with musical instruments.
options.note: The psalm names lifted hands, not clapping.

quiz.q: How does verse 3 describe the LORD who blesses His people?

options.text: The Maker of heaven and earth.
options.note: This exact title appears in verse 3, tying cosmic power to covenant blessing.

options.text: The God of Abraham alone.
options.note: That title appears elsewhere in Scripture, but verse 3 specifically names Him Maker of heaven and earth.

options.text: The Rock of Israel's fathers.
options.note: This title is not used in this short psalm.

options.text: A distant, unnamed deity.
options.note: The psalm names the LORD directly and specifically, not vaguely.

quiz.q: What is Psalm 134's place among the Psalms?

options.text: It is the last of the fifteen Songs of Ascents.
options.note: It closes the pilgrim collection running from Psalm 120 to 134.

options.text: It is the opening psalm of the whole Psalter.
options.note: Psalm 1 opens the Psalter; Psalm 134 closes the Ascents collection.

options.text: It belongs to the imprecatory psalms of judgment.
options.note: The psalm is a call to blessing, not an imprecation against enemies.

options.text: It is one of the acrostic psalms.
options.note: Psalm 145, not 134, is the acrostic psalm in this section.

quiz.q: What New Testament truth most directly extends this psalm's picture of unceasing night worship?

options.text: Believers are called a holy priesthood offering a sacrifice of praise continually.
options.note: 1 Peter 2:5, 9 and Hebrews 13:15 extend exactly this calling to all believers in Christ.

options.text: Only ordained priests may worship God at all.
options.note: The New Testament extends priestly worship to all believers, not to a restricted class.

options.text: Worship should only happen once a year at a festival.
options.note: The psalm itself calls for worship beyond festival hours, into the night; the New Testament calls for unceasing praise.

options.text: Physical temples remain the only valid place of worship.
options.note: The New Testament shows Christ as greater than the temple, opening worship beyond any single building.

questions: This psalm calls the temple's unseen night watch to keep blessing the LORD. What unseen, unnoticed part of your week could become an act of worship if you saw it that way?
Verse 2 pictures worship as lifted hands toward the sanctuary. What would it look like for you to bring a physical, bodily posture of surrender into your own prayer or worship this week?
The psalm ends with a blessing flowing back from God to His worshipers. Where in your life have you experienced God's blessing following your own worship of Him, even in a hard season?
Believers are now called a holy priesthood offering praise continually. What would change in your daily rhythm if you treated even ordinary tasks as part of that continual sacrifice of praise?
The psalm closes the Songs of Ascents with worship that never stops for the night. What practice could help you carry worship into the quieter, less visible hours of your own life?

translationNotes.note: Servants who serve by night likely refers to Levites and priests keeping watch over the sanctuary through the night hours, as prescribed in passages such as 1 Chronicles 9:27.
Lift up your hands renders a gesture of prayer and praise widespread in ancient Israelite worship, an outward posture of dependence and adoration toward God.
Maker of heaven and earth is a title used elsewhere to open acts of blessing, Genesis 14:19, 22, tying the LORD's cosmic power directly to His covenant care for His worshiping people.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via web_fetch from biblehub.com/bsb/psalms/134.htm (Berean Standard Bible), verified against the section map bounds Psalm 134:1-3. No em or en dashes present; none appeared in the source verses. No verified sermon found this session from the trusted cluster specifically expounding Psalm 134 as its primary text, so sermon status is none.
